originalTitle: New outbreak of Ebola kills 65 in eastern DR Congo
title: Tiny Bugs Making Friends Sick in Congo! But Helpers Are There!
content:
Hello from Africa! Your Friendly News Reporter is Here!

Hi everyone! It's your friendly news reporter here, bringing you important news from our big, beautiful continent, Africa! Today, we're talking about something very, very tiny that is making some of our friends in a country called the Democratic Republic of Congo feel unwell. Don't worry, lots of smart and brave people are working super hard to help everyone feel better!

What is This Tiny Bug?

Imagine a tiny, tiny bug, so small you can't even see it without a special powerful magnifying glass. This tiny bug is called Ebola. It's a type of germ that can make people very sick. It's not like a normal cold or flu, it's a bit more serious. But remember, it's very rare and doctors know how to help. Did you know? Germs like Ebola are called viruses! They are like little invisible sprites that can make you sneeze or cough or feel tired if they get inside your body.

Where is Our Friend, Congo?

The Democratic Republic of Congo, or DR Congo for short, is a really big country right here in Africa! It has beautiful forests, amazing animals, and lots of friendly people. It's a place full of life and nature. Our friends there are strong and brave. Did you know? DR Congo is one of the largest countries in Africa! It's so big, you could fit many other countries inside it!

What Happens When People Get Sick?

When someone gets this tiny Ebola bug inside them, they might start to feel very tired. They might get a fever, which means their body feels very hot. They could have a tummy ache or feel like they want to be sick. It's important that if someone feels like this, they get help from a doctor or a nurse right away. Sadly, because this bug is very strong, some people who got sick in DR Congo couldn't get better. About 65 people became too unwell and passed away, which makes us very sad. But many more people are getting help and feeling better, and that's good news.

Superheroes in Special Suits!

Guess what? There are amazing superheroes working in DR Congo right now! They are doctors, nurses, and health helpers. These brave people wear special protective suits, like astronauts or knights in shining armour. These suits help keep the tiny bugs away from them so they can safely help the sick people. They work day and night, giving medicine and care to everyone who needs it. They are truly heroes! Did you know? The special suits doctors wear when they are helping people with Ebola are called Personal Protective Equipment, or PPE for short! They cover them from head to toe!

How Can We Stay Safe?

Even though this is happening far away for most of us, there are always good rules to remember to stay healthy and safe from all kinds of germs: 1. **Wash Your Hands!** This is super important! Use soap and water and scrub-a-dub-dub for at least 20 seconds, which is like singing "Happy Birthday" twice! This washes away all those tiny invisible bugs. 2. **Don't Touch Your Face!** Try not to touch your eyes, nose, or mouth with unwashed hands. This stops germs from jumping into your body. 3. **Stay Away When Someone is Sick!** If a friend or family member is sick, it's best to give them some space so their germs don't spread to you. Did you know? Washing your hands properly is one of the best ways to stop germs from spreading! It's like having tiny superpowers against bugs!

Brave Doctors and Helpers

Doctors and health workers are not just giving medicine; they are also teaching people in DR Congo how to stay safe. They are showing them the best ways to wash hands and how to take care of sick family members so that the tiny bug doesn't spread to others. They are making sure everyone knows what to do. There are about 246 people in DR Congo who have gotten sick with this tiny bug. But remember, each one of these people is getting help from our amazing superhero doctors and nurses. They are getting lots of care and love.

Working Together to Feel Better

Lots of people and groups from all over Africa and the world are working together to help DR Congo. They are sending doctors, nurses, medicine, and clean water. When we all work together, we can make a big difference and help our friends get better. Did you know? Sometimes, doctors can give a special shot called a vaccine. It's like a training exercise for your body, teaching it how to fight off certain tiny bugs before you even get sick! There are special vaccines being used to help fight Ebola too!

A Message of Hope

It's sad to hear about people getting sick, but it's also important to know that there's lots of hope! People in DR Congo are strong, and they have many helpers by their side. We can all send them happy thoughts and remember to keep ourselves healthy by washing our hands and being kind to everyone. Everyone deserves to be healthy and happy! Remember, your friendly news reporter from Africa will always bring you the news, and always remind you to be kind and stay safe! Until next time!
summary: Some people in the Democratic Republic of Congo are getting sick from a tiny germ called Ebola, but brave doctors and helpers are working hard to make everyone better and keep others safe.
whyMatters: This is important because it tells us about a serious germ affecting people in an African country, and it teaches us how doctors are helping and how we can all stay safe from germs by washing our hands.

originalTitle: Former Nigerian minister sentenced to 75 years in rare corruption verdict
title: Playing Fair: A Big Rule for Everyone in Nigeria!
content:
Hi there, amazing friends! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, bringing you super important news from our wonderful continent, Africa. Today, we're zooming into a very important country called Nigeria! It's a land full of vibrant colours, amazing music, and super kind people.

What is a 'Minister'?

Imagine your school has a head teacher who helps make sure everything runs smoothly, right? Well, a country also has special helpers called 'ministers'. They have very big and important jobs. Each minister is in charge of a different part of helping the country. Some ministers help with schools, some with hospitals, and some, like the one we're talking about today, help with 'power'.

What is 'Power' for a Country?

When we talk about 'power' in a country, we're talking about electricity! You know, the magic stuff that turns on your lights so you can read your favourite storybook? The stuff that makes the fridge cold so your food stays fresh? And the power that helps doctors use special machines in hospitals to make people better? That's electricity! It's super important for everyone. Did you know? Nigeria is the most populated country in Africa! That means lots and lots of people need electricity for their homes, schools, and businesses.

A Special Job, A Big Trust

So, a 'Power Minister' has a very, very important job. Their job is to make sure everyone in Nigeria gets the electricity they need. They also make sure the money meant for electricity projects is used wisely and fairly. It's a job that needs a lot of trust, like when your parents trust you to look after your little sibling or to share your toys fairly.

When Someone Doesn't Play Fair

Now, sometimes, sad things happen when someone doesn't play fair. Imagine if your friend was given money to buy yummy snacks for everyone in the class, but they kept some of the money for themselves and bought fewer snacks, or snacks that weren't as good. That wouldn't be fair, would it? It would make everyone feel a bit sad and cheated. This is a bit like what happened with a former Power Minister in Nigeria, a man named Saleh Mamman. He had a big job, looking after money meant to help bring electricity to many places. But the news tells us that he didn't play fair with that money. This is what we call 'corruption'. Corruption is when people use money that belongs to everyone for themselves, instead of using it for good things like lights for your homes or equipment for hospitals.

The Rules and the Court

Just like your school has rules to make sure everyone plays fair, a country also has very important rules. And when someone breaks those rules, especially big ones like taking money that doesn't belong to them, there are special places called 'courts'. A court is like a big referee in a game. Judges, who are like super-smart referees, listen to all the stories and look at all the clues to decide if someone broke the rules.

The Judges Made a Decision

After looking at everything very carefully, the judges in Nigeria made a very important decision. They said, "Yes, Mr. Saleh Mamman did not play fair. He broke the rules about the country's money." This means he was found 'guilty'. It's like when the referee blows the whistle and says, "Foul!" because someone didn't follow the game's rules.

A Big Consequence

Because not playing fair with the country's money is a very, very serious thing, the judges decided on a very big 'consequence'. They said he should go to jail for 75 years. Wow! That's a super, super long time! It's like having your birthday 75 times! This big number shows everyone how important it is to be honest and play fair, especially when you have a big job helping the country. Did you know? Justice systems, with courts and judges, are found in almost every country in the world! They help make sure rules are followed and everyone is treated fairly.

A Little Mystery

Now, here's a little mystery part. Even though the judges made this big decision, no one knows exactly where Mr. Mamman is right now. It's like playing hide-and-seek, but in a very serious way. The people who help run the country, called the 'authorities', are looking for him so that the court's decision can happen. They want to make sure that rules are always followed, no matter who you are.

Why is This Important for YOU?

"Why does this news matter to me?" you might ask. Well, it matters a lot! When money that should be used for good things, like building more schools, making sure hospitals have enough medicine, or bringing electricity to more homes so you can study at night, gets taken by someone, it hurts everyone. When people play fair and use money wisely, our country, Nigeria, gets stronger and brighter! More schools get built, more people get healthy, and more homes have lights! This means you, and all your friends, have even better chances to learn, play, and grow up in a super amazing country.

Being Honest and Strong

This news shows that even when big people make mistakes, there are brave people working hard to make things right. It tells us that honesty is super important, and that rules are there to protect everyone, especially children like you. It's a reminder that everyone, no matter how big or small their job, must always play fair and be honest. This makes our communities stronger and our future brighter! Let's all promise to always play fair!
summary: A former important helper in Nigeria, called a Power Minister, was found to have not played fair with the country's money and was told he should go to jail, showing that being honest and fair is very important for everyone.
whyMatters: This news matters because it shows that rules are important for everyone, even people with big jobs. When people play fair, money can be used to build schools, hospitals, and bring lights to homes, which helps all children in Nigeria have a better life.

originalTitle: 'They shot my neighbour in the head' - the lakeside city traumatised by war
title: Friends for Uvira: A Story of Hope!
content:
Hello, my wonderful friends! I'm your super news reporter, and I'm here to tell you a story about a very special place in Africa called Uvira. Uvira is a city in a big, beautiful country called the Democratic Republic of Congo, or DR Congo for short. It's right next to a super big lake called Lake Tanganyika. Imagine a city by a huge, sparkling blue lake! It sounds amazing, right?

A Beautiful City by a Big Lake

Uvira is like many towns and cities you know. It has homes, schools, and markets where people buy delicious food and colorful clothes. Children play games in the sunshine, and families laugh together. It's a busy place with lots of friendly faces. People love living by the lake because it's so pretty, and sometimes they even catch fish for dinner! Did you know? Lake Tanganyika is the second deepest lake in the *whole world*! It's so big, it looks like an ocean! But sometimes, in different parts of the world, even in lovely places like Uvira, grown-ups don't always get along. It's a bit like when friends have a disagreement on the playground. Sometimes, these disagreements can become very big and make people feel sad and scared.

When Friends Don't Play Nicely

A little while ago, something happened in Uvira that made many people very worried. There were some groups of people, a bit like teams, who were not playing nicely with each other. We call them "rebel fighters" and "troops." They were having a very big disagreement, and it made a lot of noise and caused a lot of trouble in the city. It wasn't a good time for Uvira. People felt scared because things were not peaceful. When grown-ups fight, it can make everyone feel very unsafe. Imagine if your classroom suddenly became very noisy and some friends were shouting. You'd feel confused and maybe a bit scared, right? That's how some people in Uvira felt. Did you know? The Democratic Republic of Congo is the second-largest country in Africa! It's so big, you could fit many other countries inside it!

Feeling Sad and Scared

Some very sad things happened during this time. People's homes were hurt, and some people got injured. It's never nice when people get hurt, and it makes everyone very, very sad. When these groups were fighting, they did things that were not kind or helpful. It made the city feel like it had a big cloud of sadness over it. Children especially felt very confused and scared. They just wanted to play and go to school, but it was hard when grown-ups were not being peaceful. It's important to remember that when bad things happen, it's okay to feel sad, angry, or scared. Grown-ups try their best to keep everyone safe.

Helping Hands and Kind Hearts

But guess what? Even when things are tough, there are always heroes! Many people in Uvira are like superheroes with kind hearts. They started helping each other. Neighbours helped neighbours, and friends looked out for friends. When someone was sad, another person gave them a hug. When someone needed food, others shared what they had. This is what we call community spirit! It means everyone working together like a big family. It shows that even after a big storm, the sun can still shine, and people can help make things better. Did you know? When you help a friend who is sad, you are showing kindness! Kindness is like a superpower that makes everyone feel better.

Working Towards Peace

After the big disagreements, many people, including very important grown-ups from other countries, came to Uvira. They wanted to help everyone stop fighting and start living in peace again. Peace means everyone is safe, happy, and can play and work without fear. It means listening to each other and solving problems with words, not with fighting. These helpers are sometimes called "peacekeepers." Their job is to help everyone calm down and talk so that no more sad things happen. They want to make sure children can go to school and play again, and families can live happily in their homes.

What Peace Feels Like

Imagine your favorite playground. When everyone is playing nicely, sharing swings, and taking turns, that's peace! Everyone is happy and having fun. That's what the people of Uvira want for their city again. They want to feel safe and calm, like a quiet morning by the lake. It takes time to fix things when big disagreements happen, but with lots of kind hearts and helping hands, Uvira can become a peaceful and happy city once more. Everyone is working hard to bring smiles back to people's faces. Did you know? Many countries around the world send special helpers to places like Uvira to help keep the peace and make sure everyone is safe.

How You Can Help (Even From Far Away!)

You might be thinking, "What can I do?" Well, you can do a lot! Even though you might be far away, you can practice being kind and peaceful in your own life. When you share your toys, listen to your friends, and use kind words instead of angry ones, you are helping to make the world a more peaceful place. Every little act of kindness makes a big difference. Think about the children in Uvira. They want to play and learn just like you. By being kind and respectful to everyone you meet, you are helping to build a better world for all of us. Let's send our happy thoughts and wishes for peace to Uvira, so that all the children there can laugh, play, and learn without any worries. Thank you for listening to this important story! We hope for bright, peaceful days ahead for Uvira.
summary: The beautiful lakeside city of Uvira in DR Congo experienced a difficult time when different groups of grown-ups fought, but now kind people are working together to bring peace and happiness back to its children and families.
whyMatters: It matters because everyone, especially children, deserves to live in a safe and peaceful place where they can play, learn, and be happy without feeling scared. It shows that even when bad things happen, people can help each other and work for peace.

originalTitle: Is the US dialling down its support for Taiwan?
title: Are America and Taiwan Still Good Friends?
content:
Hello, Super Reporters!

Hi everyone! It's your friendly news reporter here, bringing you stories from all around the world! Today, we're going on a special trip far, far away, to learn about some countries and their friendships. It's like when you have best friends at school, but on a much bigger scale with whole countries! Sometimes, countries are good friends and they help each other. Other times, they might have little disagreements, just like you and your friends sometimes do. Today's story is about America, a big country, and Taiwan, a smaller island far away, and another really big country called China.

Meet Our Friends: America, Taiwan, and China!

First, let's meet our main characters! Imagine a huge, huge country called **America**. It's very far from us here in Africa, across a big ocean. America is known for many things, like tall buildings and lots of different people. Then, there's **China**. China is also a super, super big country, even bigger than America in some ways, and it has lots and lots of people. It's known for amazing history and beautiful places. And then there's **Taiwan**. Taiwan is a much smaller place. It's an island, which means it's a piece of land surrounded by water, just like some islands you might know. Taiwan is very close to China. Did you know? America is so big, it has many different kinds of weather, from sunny beaches to snowy mountains!

Why Are Countries Friends?

Just like you help your friends, countries help each other too! They might share things, trade goods, or even promise to help if one friend is in trouble. This is called 'support'. When one country 'supports' another, it means they are helping them be strong and safe. America has often been a very good friend to Taiwan. They have helped Taiwan stay safe and grow. It's like a big brother helping a smaller sister on the playground. They want Taiwan to be happy and safe. Did you know? Leaders from different countries often meet to talk about how they can be good friends and help each other. They shake hands and share ideas!

Taiwan and China: A Little Disagreement

Now, here's where it gets a little tricky, but we can understand it! Imagine two kids, China and Taiwan, and they are looking at a toy. China says, "That toy belongs to me! You are part of my toys!" But Taiwan says, "No, I am my own toy! I want to play by myself!" This is a very simple way to think about it. China believes that Taiwan is not a separate country but is actually part of China. Taiwan, however, believes it is its own place, with its own leaders and its own way of doing things. This has been a disagreement for a very long time. Did you know? An island is a piece of land completely surrounded by water. Africa is a continent, which is a very, very big piece of land!

America's Job: Helping Taiwan

For many years, America has been a friend to Taiwan. Because Taiwan is much smaller than China, America has helped Taiwan feel safe. One way America has helped is by sometimes selling things that can help Taiwan protect itself. These things are called 'arms' or 'weapons'.

What Are "Arms" or "Weapons"?

When we say 'arms' or 'weapons', we don't mean scary monsters or things for fighting all the time. Think of it like this: if you have a dog, you might have a strong fence to keep it safe in your yard, right? Or if you have a bike, you might have a helmet to protect your head. 'Arms' are things that countries use to protect themselves from harm. They are used for defense, to keep their people and their land safe. Did you know? Police officers and soldiers sometimes use special equipment to help keep people safe and to protect their country. This equipment helps them do their important jobs!

When America's Old Leader Visited China

A few years ago, the leader of America was a man named Donald Trump. He was like the chief of America. One day, Mr. Trump went on a special trip to visit China. When important leaders visit other countries, they talk about many things. They talk about how to be friends, how to share things, and how to make the world a better place. Did you know? When leaders travel to other countries, they often fly in special airplanes, and they meet with the leaders of those countries to have important discussions!

No Promise for Taiwan?

Now, here's the important news part. When Mr. Trump was visiting China, everyone wondered if he would promise to keep selling 'arms' to Taiwan. Remember, America had been helping Taiwan with these things to stay safe. But during his visit, Mr. Trump did *not* promise to sell more arms to Taiwan. He didn't say "yes." This means he didn't give a clear promise that America would continue to help Taiwan in that way, at that very moment. Did you know? Making a promise is a very important thing. It means you say you will definitely do something. When a leader doesn't make a promise, it means they are still thinking or deciding.

What Does This Mean for Taiwan?

So, what happens when a big friend like America doesn't promise to help in a certain way? Taiwan, the smaller island, might feel a little bit worried. It's like if your big brother usually helps you with your homework, and then one day he says, "Hmm, I'm not sure if I can help you today." It makes Taiwan wonder if America is still its strong helper and friend, especially when it comes to staying safe from disagreements with China. It made Taiwan think, "Is America still on my side as much as before?" Did you know? Feeling safe is very important for everyone, including countries. When countries feel safe, their people can live happily and grow!

Why Is This News Important?

This story helps us understand how countries are connected. Even though America, China, and Taiwan are far away from us, their friendships and disagreements can make a difference in the world. When big countries make decisions, it can change how smaller countries feel and what they do. It shows us that even big countries have to think carefully about who their friends are and how they help them. This event was a moment when America's actions made Taiwan wonder about their friendship.

Stay Tuned!

That's our news story for today, young reporters! Remember, learning about the world helps us understand how everyone is connected, just like a big global family. Keep asking questions and keep learning! See you next time!
summary: America's old leader, Donald Trump, visited China and didn't promise to sell weapons to Taiwan, making Taiwan wonder if America's strong support was changing.
whyMatters: This is important because when big countries change how they help their friends, it can make smaller countries feel worried about their safety and how strong their friendships are in the world.

content: In a league dominated by Celtic and Rangers, referees are always accused of favouring one or the other. And that means intense pressure for officials in Scotland.
